_comment: Source: Michigan MDHHS Section 9-17R Cefazolin (Revised 8/11/2023, MDHHS Reviewed 2023). Base Michigan template.
protocol_reference: Michigan Medication Protocol Section 9-17R: Cefazolin — MDHHS Approved 8/11/2023, Reviewed 2023
indications: Major soft tissue injuries with contamination risk
contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to cefazolin or other cephalosporins
Severe allergy to penicillin (cross-reactivity possible — use ceftriaxone only as alternative; consult online MC if uncertain)
precautions: Infusion (100 mL NS over 15-30 minutes) is CONTRAINDICATED for patients <7 years old — use slow IVP instead
Confirm allergy history before administration; penicillin cross-allergy risk is low but exists
Reconstitute per kit instructions before administering
expected_effects: Prophylactic antimicrobial coverage against gram-positive organisms (Staphylococcus, Streptococcus) in open injuries
notes: Dilution for slow IVP: reconstitute 2 grams in 20 mL NS = 100 mg/mL solution
Adult administration: 2 grams slow IVP over 3-5 minutes OR infusion in 100 mL NS over 15-30 minutes
Pediatric <7 years: slow IVP only — infusion contraindicated
Ceftriaxone (9-18R) is the alternative antibiotic for same indications

dosing.population: Adult (>14 yr) — open fracture / amputation / major soft tissue
dosing.dose: 2 grams slow IVP over 3-5 minutes OR 2 grams in 100 mL NS infusion over 15-30 minutes
dosing.notes: Reconstitute: 2 g in 20 mL NS = 100 mg/mL; use slow IVP or infusion per available supplies

dosing.dose: 30 mg/kg; maximum 2 grams; slow IVP over 3-5 min OR infusion in 100 mL NS over 15-30 min
dosing.notes: Reconstitute same as adult; infusion permitted ≥7 years

dosing.dose: 30 mg/kg; maximum 2 grams; slow IVP only
dosing.notes: Infusion (100 mL NS) CONTRAINDICATED for patients <7 years — slow IVP only

used_in_protocols: Trauma — Open Fracture / Amputation / Major Soft Tissue Injury
